narrative:
When the last summer of his third decade of life hit, the 35‑year‑old man—who most afternoons spent hauling heavy boxes in a downtown warehouse—felt a vague, uncomfortable cramp in his lower abdomen that he chalked up to “overworking” and the city’s endless traffic. He had been battling ulcerative colitis (UC) for eight years, a history that began when a rash of bloody, frothy stools had abruptly cut back a routine that once seemed trivial. The diagnosis had come with a set of pills—mesalamine and a short course of steroids—followed by a plateau of quiescent periods punctuated by “flare‑ups” that he endured by doing what he thought best: skip doses whenever he was on the go, let his stress at work dictate his diet, and, when necessary, take a handful of over‑the‑counter NSAIDs for the lingering back pain that never quite budged.

The flare that began with the abdominal cramp was no ordinary flareup. In the ensuing days he reported a new pattern of bowel movements that was frequent yet unpredictable. At first, he told his friends that perhaps he had caught a cold: the stools were mixed green and brown, with a faint tingling in the rectal area that he mistook for a "strep throat" that never came. The first time he went to the office of his PCP, a family nurse practitioner, the symptoms were mild. His vital signs looked normal. A complete blood count returned with a hemoglobin of 10.9 g/dL, a microcytic picture that his doctor had long associated with his known iron deficiency anemia secondary to chronic UC. The patient was already on oral iron and monthly B12 injections, a combination that kept him from the full-blown pallor that would otherwise have followed.

“What’s the plan? Keep doing your meds, eat iron‑rich foods? Anything else?” the practitioner asked.

The young man laughed, rolled his eyes, and said, “I don’t have the time or the money for these appointments. I’m good.”

He left the office with a prescription for an extended‑release mesalamine but was, again, unlikely to fill it. The week progressed, and he found himself having two to three bloody stools each day. The pain that had started as a cramp migrated to a deeper level of the abdomen, a dull ache that was sometimes sharp when he forced himself up from an overturned chair. He did what most people do in such circumstances: he tried to “drink more water,” eat “well,” and, when his job’s hours became too demanding, took diclofenac from a friend who had chronic back pain.

Three weeks after the initial cramp, he found himself in a hurry to the emergency department after a sudden, 12‑hour bout of dehydration, low‑grade fevers (not more than 101 °F), and a sudden spike in the frequency of stools—up to 12 times a day—with thick, bright‑red clots that made his clothes feel sticky. The nursing staff noted his blood pressure 95/57 mmHg, pulse 110 bpm, respiratory rate 22, and a low central venous pressure.

The ED physicians ran a full panel: CBC, CMP, CRP, ESR, coagulation studies, and a stool series for C. difficile toxin and bacterial pathogens. The results were telling. Hemoglobin was 9.8 g/dL, WBC count 18.6 K/µL with a shift to the left, CRP 38 mg/L, ESR 94 mm/hr. The stool toxins came back negative, the cultures were bland. The picture fit a severe flare of ulcerative colitis, with a hint of a superimposed infection—a possibility that was being considered as the treatment plan unfolded.

The attending intensivist, noticing the patient’s comorbidities, specifically the documented primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) with a mild rise in alkaline phosphatase a couple of months earlier (now 280 U/L, 1.5 times the upper limit of normal), wanted to double‑check the biliary tree. At the same time, the patient’s known calcium oxalate kidney stones had recurred in the summer of the previous year; the patient had endured a painful flank ache that had lingered for a week, leading him to a urologist who discovered a 7 mm proximal ureteric stone on an ultrasound. In the ED, they decided to monitor his urine output, give him high‑dose IV fluids, and start him on a cautious hydration plan with a clear fluid input. The decision for a CT abdomen/pelvis without contrast was urgent, as the attending physician suspected worsening colitis with a possible perforation.

The CT report described diffuse colonic wall thickening (12 mm of the sigmoid, 10 mm the descending) with a pericolic fat stranding consistent with severe inflammatory changes, but nothing beyond that; no free air, no abscess formation. The kidneys were normal aside from the known stone in the right proximal ureter, causing mild hydroureter but no obstruction to the point of an emergency intervention.

The ED team, aware of the patient’s high thrombo‑embolic risk due to systemic inflammation (C‑reactive protein so high that it could tip the balance towards a hypercoagulable state) and his recent prolonged sedentary shift, decided on a prophylactic low‑molecular‑weight heparin protocol pending discharge.

The next morning, after a slow improvement in hydration, the patient was admitted to the Gastroenterology service. In the hospital, his flares were escalated with a course of intravenous methylprednisolone 125 mg every 8 hours, a fluid resuscitation at 1.5 × 10^9 mL, and a high‑dose oral mesalamine 4.8 g daily. The blood work showed a gradual downtrend in CRP but the hemoglobin kept dipping, falling to 8.3 g/dL by day four, requiring two packed red blood cell units. He also required a fresh frozen plasma infusion because his coagulation profile slowly worsened (INR 1.4, fibrinogen 80 mg/dL).

At day five, a flexible sigmoidoscopy was performed. The endoscopic impression was an ulcerated, friable mucosa throughout the sigmoid and mid‑colon. Biopsies confirmed active colitis with crypt abscess formation and occasional pseudomembranes; no viral cytopathic changes were seen. The pathologist noted the presence of iron‑laden macrophages reflecting his chronic iron deficiency. No dysplasia was detected, but because of his PSC status and pancolitis, a colonoscopy had already been scheduled for every 6 months as part of surveillance, an appointment he had missed twice over the past two years.

The day after the endoscopy, the patient complained of worsening abdominal pain during the night; his abdomen became rigid, rebound tenderness was present, and the temperature spiked to 103 °F. A repeat CT scan was ordered—this time with contrast. It now revealed a small area of gas within the portal venous system, a harbinger of portal vein thrombosis, and a faint collection of free fluid in the right paracolic gutter. No perforation was seen yet. The patient was immediately switched to intravenous antibiotics: vancomycin to cover gram‑positive organisms, cefepime for anaerobes and gram‑negative coverage, and metronidazole was dropped.

He remained ICU‑level for monitoring, but a few days later, on day eight, the patient’s pain localized to the flank and radiated up into the back. The pain was sharp, worsened when he breathed, and did not correlate with constipation or bowel habits. An ultrasound of the abdomen showed the stone had migrated into the left kidney's lower pole, causing hydronephrosis. A urology consult was called; the patient was placed on a percutaneous nephrostomy tube, which relieved the pain and stabilized the urinary output. A 24‑hour urine collection later revealed a high urinary oxalate level (12 mg/kg in a day), a pattern he had had before, indicating the chronic tendency to form calcium oxalate stones.

The story takes a darker turn on day nine: a chest X‑ray taken for a persistent cough revealed a subtle wedge‑shaped opacity in the left lower lobe. A CT pulmonary angiogram was performed; it confirmed a large saddle pulmonary embolism. By this point the patient had already had a deep vein thrombosis in the left popliteal artery a month prior, diagnosed when he had a sudden onset of calf pain during a day shift. That DVT had been treated with a 6‑month course of rivaroxaban, which he had stopped a week before because of the insurance co‑payment after his last prescription.

He was placed on a high‑dose unfractionated heparin drip, and his anticoagulation management was shifted to a combination of therapeutic LMWH and then back to rivaroxaban after stabilization. The ICU team closely watched his anticoagulation levels and his overall hemodynamic status, which had been precarious due to systemic inflammation and repeated fluctuations in blood pressure.

At day ten, a sudden worsening of tachycardia, a rise of his lactate to 3.6 mmol/L, and a new drop in SpO2 from 98% to 85% in the presence of a flat left chest wall brought about emergency surgical consultation. The surgeons felt a perforation was highly probable: the peritoneal signs he had in the middle of that day indicated sudden peritoneal contamination. He was taken to the operating room, where an emergency right colectomy was performed. A perforated ulcer measuring 4 cm was found in the proximal ascending colon, with a focal abscess and purulent fluid seen throughout the right paracolic gutter. The surgeons resected the proximal colon, closed the distal ileum with a stapled side‑to‑side anastomosis, and left the perforation in the stump for drainage (a Hartmann’s procedure).

Post‑operative management was a marathon. He was admitted to the ICU on ventilatory support for a complicated respiratory effort that had already been aggravated by a large pulmonary embolism and a massive fluid shift. They were able to extubate after 48 hours, but he needed a non‑invasive ventilator for a short period because of residual respiratory compromise. Blood cultures grew E. coli, for which they added piperacillin‑tazobactam. He required a second blood transfusion on day 12 to maintain his hemoglobin above 9 g/dL. His WBC count peaked at 25 K/µL, and his CRP spiked again to 60 mg/L at this time.

His metabolic panel, however, showed a rising bilirubin up to 2.3 mg/dL and a plateau in alkaline phosphatase at 420 U/L—an ominous sign in someone with PSC. The attending gastroenterologist was already preparing a plan for an induction of infliximab—a biologic agent that requires a maintenance dose every two weeks—and discussed the possibility of a prophylactic mesenchymal stem cell infusion, a therapy that was still experimental in UC but had shown promise. The patient, exhausted and physically reduced, was hesitant but ultimately agreed to a trial after a discussion with his insurance.

A week after the surgery—day 17—he began the first infusion of infliximab 5 mg/kg. The next dose was scheduled after two weeks. He received iron infusions and started a high‑protein diet with vitamin B12 supplementation because his serum B12 remained low at 180 pg/mL.

Despite the aggressive management, the patient was plagued by a “new” rash: a diffuse, itchy purpuric eruption that appeared on his lower limbs on day 21. He was sent to dermatology as a potential drug reaction to either the biologic or the prophylactic anticoagulation. The skin biopsy confirmed leukocytoclastic vasculitis—an autoimmune phenomenon possibly triggered by the inflammatory cascade of UC and the potent immunosuppressive effect of infliximab.

The infection was being managed; however, another complication loomed. The patient’s creatinine climbed to 2.5 mg/dL, probably related to the nephrotoxic potential of the high‑dose antibiotics paired with a recent nephrostomy placement. The renal team decided to dial off certain antibiotics and switch to a more favorable regimen to minimize further renal insults. Meanwhile, his potassium began to rise; he was on a low‑potassium diet, but the kidneys weren’t keeping up.

Because of the PSC and the extensive pancolitis, the gastroenterology team re‑ordered a colonoscopy at day 27 to assess for dysplasia or neoplastic lesions. It was conducted with a 15 mm polypectomy for an ileal stricture visible on a flexible sigmoidoscopy. The pathology returned with high‑grade dysplasia in the polyp. The team consulted a surgical oncologist, who suggested a subtotal colectomy to reduce long‑term cancer risk. The patient’s condition—now complicated by ongoing sepsis from the perforation recovery, a significant bleed risk with anticoagulation, and the new high‑grade dysplasia—did not lend itself to a smooth surgery, but the decision to proceed was made in late December after a family meeting.

On day 29, after a 2‑hour wait for the patient's platelet count to stabilize (it had dipped to 62 × 10^9/L), the patient was taken back to surgery. This time, the operation was a subtotal colectomy with a loop ileostomy. The procedure took seven hours, with significant blood loss (~1200 mL). After revascularization of the remaining ileum, the surgery concluded.

The postoperative period was rough. His leukocyte count spiked again to 30 K/µL, his CRP at 70 mg/L, and he developed a fever that resolved on the third postoperative day with vancomycin. The abdominal cavity was irrigated with warm saline, but some residual abscesses in the right upper quadrant persisted and were drained via a percutaneous approach.

On day 35, the patient’s renal function improved slightly after switching to a low‑dose furosemide regimen. He was weaned off the ventilator, and the nephrostomy tube was removed two days later as imaging showed no hydronephrosis. The skin rash resolved spontaneously over the next week.

The story didn't end there. In early February, after five months of total parenteral nutrition to mitigate malabsorption caused by extensive resections, the patient was finally able to tolerate a bland diet and a progressive oral schedule. His weight had been decreasing for the past several months, from 82 kg to 56 kg, but over the last five weeks, he started gaining back about 2 kg.

A follow‑up colonoscopy on day 110 found a large, irregular polyp at the ileocolic anastomosis. The pathology was consistent with a colorectal adenocarcinoma, moderately differentiated, stage pT3N1M0. The surgical oncologist recommended a low‑dose chemotherapeutic regimen in addition to a planned extended ileocolic resection in six months. The patient, exhausted, agreed. His condition had dramatically worsened due to repeated flares, septic complications, and now cancer. The narrative, however, does not end with an abrupt tragedy. The last entry—date 2026‑03‑25—shows our patient at home, in hospice care, reflecting on how he has, through the last several years, endured a cascade of medical crises. The story ends with a note about his decision to focus on quality of life, emphasizing that the disease course had been relentless, with each flare eclipsing the last, leaving a chronicle of relentless worsening that, despite aggressive interventions, remained ultimately unresolved.

timeline.reason_for_visit: Abdominal cramp and mild bloody stools
timeline.note:
Chief Complaint: Vague lower abdominal cramp.
History of Present Illness: 35‑year‑old male presents with intermittent lower abdominal cramping, frequent loose stools, and greenish mucus discharge for the past few days. No fever, vomiting, or weight loss reported. He denies recent travel or dietary changes.
Past Medical History: Inflammatory Bowel Disease–associated anemia, iron deficiency anemia, vitamin B12 deficiency, primary sclerosing cholangitis (mild elevation), colorectal cancer risk, venous thromboembolism, calcium oxalate kidney stones.
Medications: None. 
Allergies: NKDA.
Physical Examination: Vitals stable – BP 110/70, HR 82, Temp 98.4°F, RR 16, O2 98%. Abdomen mild tenderness in the right lower quadrant without rebound or guarding. Bowel sounds normal. Cardiovascular, respiratory, and extremity exams unremarkable.
Labs: CBC shows Hb 10.9 g/dL, WBC 6.8k/µL. CRP 5 mg/L. CMP within normal limits.
Assessment: Likely flare of underlying inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn’s disease or ulcerative colitis) presenting with abdominal cramp, mucus in stool, and mild inflammation. Differential includes infectious colitis, diverticulitis, appendicitis, functional abdominal pain, and colonic polyps.
Plan:
1. Initiate extended‑release mesalamine 2.4 g PO daily (symptomatic therapy for IBD flare). Counsel patient on importance of adherence; discuss potential alternatives (e.g., sulfasalazine) if cost or compliance is an issue.
2. Symptom management: Stay hydrated, avoid potential gut irritants (spicy, fried foods), consider low‑residue diet if stool frequency is high.
3. Address anemia: start oral iron supplement ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily; advise monitoring for GI side effects.
4. Monitor disease activity: CBC and CRP repeated in 4 weeks. Consider fecal calprotectin if symptoms persist after 2–4 weeks.
5. Follow‑up: Return to clinic in 4 weeks for reassessment; if symptoms worsen or show signs of peritonitis, call office or go to ER promptly.
6. Referral: Gastroenterology consultation if inadequate response to mesalamine or if new GI symptoms develop.
7. Colon cancer surveillance: Schedule colonoscopy in 1 year per colorectal cancer risk protocol.
8. Educate patient on recognizing red‑flag symptoms (fever, worsening pain, blood per rectum, weight loss).
timeline.visit_scenario: Patient presents to the office with a vague cramp in his lower abdomen. Examination is reassuring with mild right lower quadrant tenderness and no peritoneal signs. CBC shows a hemoglobin of 10.9 g/dL, mild inflammatory markers. The clinician discusses the importance of medication adherence and prescribes an extended‑release mesalamine. The patient declines to fill the prescription, citing time and cost concerns.
timeline.patient_response: Patient says he will try, but seems reluctant to fill the prescription.
timeline.disease_progression_notes: Baseline UC flare suspected; patient non‑adherent to therapy; monitoring advised.

timeline.note:
Chief Complaint: Severe bloody diarrhea and dehydration.

History of Present Illness: 35‑year‑old male presents to the ED with 12 episodes of bright red blood per day, low‑grade fever (99.9 °F), abdominal pain, and signs of dehydration. Patient reports intermittent lower abdominal cramping the preceding day. He admits to non‑adherence with extended‑release mesalamine 2.4 g PO daily. Vitals on arrival: BP 95/57 mmHg, HR 110 bpm, RR 22 /min, O₂ sat 96 % RA, temperature 99.9 °F. Physical exam reveals thready pulses, pale conjunctiva, abdominal tenderness without guarding, and digital rectal exam shows fresh blood.

Labs: hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, WBC 18.6 k/µL, CRP 38 mg/L, ESR 94 mm/hr, PT/INR 1.2. Stool C. diff toxin and stool cultures are pending. CT abdomen/pelvis (non‑contrast) shows diffuse colonic wall thickening 10‑12 mm without perforation and a 7 mm proximal ureteric stone.

Assessment: 1. Severe flare of inflammatory bowel disease (likely ulcerative colitis) presenting with massive bloody diarrhea, anemia, leukocytosis, and elevated inflammatory markers, precipitated by medication non‑adherence. 2. Dehydration and mild sepsis. 3. Incidental 7 mm proximal ureteric stone; likely asymptomatic but warrants monitoring.

Plan:
1. Admit to gastroenterology service.
2. Initiate IV methylprednisolone 125 mg q8h for 3 days, then taper based on clinical response.
3. Continue high‑dose oral mesalamine 4.8 g daily until discharge.
4. Begin IV fluid resuscitation: 1.5 L bolus, maintenance 125 mL/hr, with urine output monitoring >1 mL/kg/hr.
5. Prophylactic LMWH 40 mg SC daily for VTE prevention.
6. Repeat CBC, CMP, CRP, ESR every 48 h.
7. Await stool culture results; consider empiric broad‑spectrum antibiotics if gram‑negative/anaerobic organisms are identified.
8. Urology consult for 7 mm proximal ureteral stone; anticipate spontaneous passage but monitor pain and renal function.
9. Discuss dietary modifications (low‑residue, adequate fluid) and strict adherence to maintenance mesalamine. 10. Counsel on signs of worsening infection or DVT.

Disposition: Observation in inpatient setting until clinical status stabilizes; prepare for possible escalation (e.g., biologic therapy) if refractory.

Follow‑up: Outpatient GI clinic in 1 week or sooner if symptoms worsen. Labs: CBC, CMP, CRP, ESR, fecal calprotectin. Continue maintenance mesalamine 2.4 g daily and ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily. Reinforce medication adherence and review dietitian and behavioral support options.

timeline.reason_for_visit: Inpatient admission for severe UC flare
timeline.note:
Chief Complaint: Severe bloody diarrhea and dehydration, ICU admission required.

History of Present Illness: 35‑y/o male with known inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) presents to the GI floor with persistent bloody diarrhea, fever (100.2 °F), abdominal pain and signs of dehydration. The patient has been on chronic high‑dose mesalamine, oral iron, monthly B12 injections, and IV methylprednisolone 125 mg q8h for acute flare. The current admission began after a severe diarrheal episode in the ED on 07/02, during which the patient received IV steroids, fluids, high‑dose mesalamine, and LMWH. On day 1 of GI floor admission, fluid resuscitation and IV iron infusion 200 mg were administered. No complications were noted.

Review of Systems: Positive for persistent bloody diarrhea, fever, abdominal pain, and signs of dehydration. No urinary symptoms, chest pain or shortness of breath.

Physical Examination: Vitals: BP 100/58, HR 104, Temp 100.2 °F, RR 20, O₂ 97% RA. General: appears ill, pale mucous membranes. Abdomen: diffuse colonic tenderness, no rebound or guarding. Digital rectal exam: bright red blood.

Labs: High inflammatory markers with CRP 35 mg/L, WBC 18.7 k/µL. Hemoglobin 9.5 g/dL, platelets 210 k/µL. Coagulation: PT/INR 1.3, INR 1.4, fibrinogen 80 mg/dL. Stool cultures bland; C. diff toxin negative.

Assessment: Exacerbation of IBD (likely ulcerative colitis flare) causing acute severe colitis with hypovolemia. Dehydration corrected with IV fluids. Steroid therapy is ongoing; IV methylpred 125 mg q8h. Mesalamine high‑dose 4.8 g PO continues. Patient’s VTE history and current steroid therapy justify LMWH prophylaxis.

Differential:
1. Severe acute ulcerative colitis flare
2. IBD flare with severe colitis despite steroids (possible steroid‑refractory)
3. Infectious colitis (C. diff ruled out, cultures bland)
4. Ischemic colitis secondary to high blood loss/dehydration (unlikely but considered)
5. PSC‑associated colitis flare
6. Medication‑induced colitis secondary to mesalamine (rare)

Plan:
1. Continue IV methylpred 125 mg q8h and taper per inpatient protocol.
2. Continue high‑dose oral mesalamine 4.8 g daily.
3. Continue LMWH 40 mg SC daily.
4. Continue IV iron 200 mg and consider repeat infusion or transition to oral iron contingent on tolerance.
5. Monitor urine output, vitals, stool frequency, and abdominal pain hourly.
6. Repeat CBC, CMP, CRP, ESR, and coagulation profile in 48 h.
7. If inadequate response after 48‑72 h of steroids, consider escalation to biologic therapy (infliximab or adalimumab) and/or tocilizumab if severe.
8. Review abdominal imaging if clinical deterioration; consider CT enterography to rule out ischemia.
9. Post‑discharge: arrange endoscopy for evaluation of mucosal healing and colorectal cancer screening per risk protocol.
10. Provide dietary counseling: high‑potassium diet, liquid meals until stool frequency improves.

Disposition: Planned for discharge after 3‑day inpatient stay pending stabilization; patient will receive outpatient follow‑up in GI clinic within 2 weeks.

Patient education: Discussed importance of adherence to steroids and mesalamine, monitoring for signs of infection or steroid adverse effects, and importance of LMWH despite discontinuation of therapy post‑discharge. Advised to seek immediate care if fever >101 °F, abdominal pain worsens, or stool >10 bloody."
timeline.visit_scenario: Patient is admitted to the GI floor. Steroids continue, fluids administered, and iron infusion is given. Coagulation profile and inflammatory markers are closely monitored. No complications noted on day 1 of admission.
timeline.patient_response: Slight improvement in stool frequency but pain persists.
timeline.disease_progression_notes: Escalation of UC flare; anemia worsening; coagulopathy trending to hypocoagulable; ICU monitoring needed.

timeline.reason_for_visit: Monitor for perforation; start broad antibiotics
timeline.note:
Chief Complaint: Acute febrile illness with peritoneal signs; concern for bowel perforation/necrotizing colitis.

History of Present Illness: The patient is a 35‑year‑old male with known ulcerative colitis (mild‑moderate) complicated by PSC and increased risk for colorectal cancer. He has been on mesalamine 2.4 g PO daily and methylprednisolone 125 mg IV q8h for the past 48 h. Today, at 22:00, he developed a sudden spike in temperature to 103.2 °F with rigid abdomen, rebound tenderness, nausea and hypotension. The patient reports 5–7 episodes of bright red stools since admission. He denies chest pain or dyspnea. No new medications or foods.

Past Medical History: IBD, PSC, VTE, iron deficiency anemia, vitamin B12 deficiency, calcium oxalate stones, IBD‑associated anemia, colorectal cancer surveillance.

Review of Systems: Positive for fever, chills, abdominal pain, nausea, decreased urinary output. Negative for chest pain, shortness of breath, orthopnea, headache.

Physical Examination: General: Pale, diaphoretic, tachypneic. Vital signs: BP 88/52 mmHg, HR 120 bpm, RR 24 /min, Temp 103.2 °F, SpO₂ 94 % RA. Abdomen: distended, rigid, with marked guarding and rebound tenderness throughout. Bowel sounds decreased. Skin warm. Cardiovascular: tachycardia, faint radial pulses. No JVD or peripheral edema. Neurologic: alert, oriented.

Labs: WBC 25 k/µL with neutrophilia, CRP 60 mg/L, INR 1.4, fibrinogen 70 mg/dL, lactate 3.2 mmol/L (pending). Blood cultures negative.

Radiology: CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast shows portal venous gas, moderate perihepatic free fluid (approx. 2 cm), and diffuse colonic wall thickening. No obvious perforation site identified.

Assessment: The presentation most consistent with a perforated viscus or necrotizing colitis secondary to severe ulcerative colitis flare, or possibly mesenteric ischemia secondary to the patient’s VTE history. Portal venous gas and free fluid raise high suspicion for bowel perforation. The patient’s hemodynamic instability mandates ICU management.

Differential Diagnosis:
1. Perforated ulcerative colitis (ischemic/necrotizing).
2. Mesenteric ischemia (arterial or venous).
3. Infectious colitis (C. difficile, bacterial).
4. Abdominal sepsis secondary to portal venous gas.
5. Transient mesenteric venous thrombosis.
6. Delayed presentation of diverticulitis.

Plan:
- Continue current broad-spectrum antibiotics (IV vancomycin 1.5 g q12h, cefepime 2 g q12h). Consider adding metronidazole 500 mg q8h pending cultures if anaerobic coverage needed.
- Steroids: Continue methylprednisolone 125 mg q8h for IBD flare; hold only if surgical definitive intervention is required.
- Fluid resuscitation: Continue 2 L NS, adjust to urine output > 0.5 mL/kg/h; consider vasopressor support if hypotension persists (norepinephrine).
- Continue LMWH continuous infusion pending repeat coagulation profile.
- Surgical consult: Immediate evaluation by general surgery for possible laparotomy and source control. Notify anesthesia for emergent operation criteria.
- Gastroenterology: Continue mesalamine 2.4 g PO daily; consider escalation if flare persists.
- ICU monitoring: Hourly vitals, urine output, continuous cardiac monitoring.
- Repeat labs: CBC, CMP, lactate, coagulation panel q6h; blood cultures repeat if clinical condition worsens.
- Imaging: Repeat CT abdomen/pelvis in 24 h or sooner if clinical deterioration.
- Nutrition: NPO, early enteral nutrition if stable post-operatively.
- VTE prophylaxis: Continue LMWH; switch to therapeutic dose if thrombus confirmed.
- Family education: Discuss potential surgical intervention, risks of bowel resection, and need for post‑operative monitoring.

Disposition: ICU admission pending surgical consult. Pending operative management.

Follow‑up: Post‑operative ICU course, step‑down to medicine floor when stable, colonoscopy per surveillance protocol within 3‑6 months. Close monitoring of CBC, iron status, and vitamin B12 levels.
timeline.visit_scenario: Patient develops a sudden spike in temperature and peritoneal signs. A contrast CT is performed revealing portal venous gas and free fluid. Broad‑spectrum antibiotics are intensified and the patient is monitored in ICU.

timeline.note:
**Chief Complaint:** Endoscopic evaluation of colitis.

**History of Present Illness:**
The patient is a 35‑year‑old male with a history of ulcerative colitis (UC), primary sclerosing cholangitis, iron deficiency anemia, B12 deficiency, and prior venous thromboembolism. He presented with persistent bloody stools, abdominal pain, and fever and has been hospitalized for a severe UC flare. On 2023‑07‑04 he was in the ICU for septic shock and portal venous gas suspicious for a perforation. The current day we performed a flexible sigmoidoscopy to assess mucosal severity.

**Physical Examination:**
- BP 94/55 mmHg, HR 118/min, Temp 102.5 °F, RR 22/min, SpO₂ 95% on room air.
- Abdomen diffuse tenderness with rebound; no obvious guarding on exam.
- Stool: 6 bright red/blood‑stained stools/day.

**Procedure Summary:**
Flexible sigmoidoscopy revealed markedly ulcerated, friable mucosa with multiple pseudomembranes. Biopsies were obtained.

**Pathology:**
- Inflammation with active colitis; no crypt architectural distortion or dysplasia.

**Laboratory Results:**
- CRP 55 mg/L (↑), WBC 22 000/µL (↑), hemoglobin 9.5 g/dL, platelets 420 000/µL, INR 1.4.
- Stool culture pending; C. difficile PCR sent.

**Assessment:**
The patient remains in a severe UC flare complicated by possible superimposed pseudomembranous colitis. The portal venous gas and free fluid raise concern for ischemia or perforation, though the bowel is not yet perforated per imaging. The patient remains hemodynamically unstable but responsive to fluid resuscitation.

**Differential Diagnosis:**
1. Severe active ulcerative colitis flare with pseudomembranous colitis.
2. Clostridioides difficile infection.
3. Ischemic colitis / early perforation indicated by portal venous gas.
4. Septic shock secondary to colitis.
5. Medication‑induced mucosal injury (high‑dose steroids/mesalamine).

**Plan:**
- **IV Methylprednisolone 125 mg q8h** – continue until clinical improvement.
- **IV vancomycin 1.5 g q12h** – continue; consider adding oral vancomycin PO for C. difficile pending PCR.
- **IV cefepime 2 g q12h** – continue for broad coverage.
- **LMWH continuous infusion** – continue for VTE prophylaxis.
- **High‑dose oral mesalamine 4.8 g daily** – continue.
- **Oral iron and monthly B12 injections** – continue.
- Monitor vitals q4h, abdominal exam q6h.
- Repeat CBC, CRP, and basic metabolic panel daily.
- Obtain abdominal CT with contrast next morning to evaluate for perforation or ischemia.
- Consult general surgery immediately; consider laparotomy if perforation confirmed.
- Consider initiating anti‑TNF therapy (infliximab) if no improvement after 48‑72 h and imaging is reassuring.

**Disposition:**
Patient remains in ICU for close monitoring. If vitals stabilize, plan a step‑down to a regular ward with continued IV steroids and antibiotics. Discharge planning contingent on resolution of systemic symptoms and mucosal healing. The patient will be followed by gastroenterology in 3‑4 days or sooner if condition changes.

**Patient Education:**
Advised about the risks of severe colitis, importance of medication adherence, signs of perforation (severe sudden pain, worsening vitals), and when to seek immediate care.

**Signature:**
[Provider Name], M.D.

timeline.note:
Chief Complaint: Right flank pain, nausea, abnormal urination.

S: Patient is a 35‑year‑old male with a history of ulcerative colitis, primary sclerosing cholangitis, venous thromboembolism, and recurrent calcium oxalate kidney stones. He reports right flank pain that has been present for the past 24 hours, associated with nausea and mild urinary frequency. He denies fever, chills, gross hematuria, or recent trauma.

O: Vitals are stable with a blood pressure of 102/58 mmHg, heart rate 110/min, temperature 98.6°F, respiratory rate 18/min, and SpO2 98% on room air. Examination reveals right flank tenderness to palpation; left flank and abdomen are otherwise benign. Peripheral pulses are intact. No abdominal guarding or rebound. Mild leukocytosis noted on CBC (WBC 9.5 ×10^9/L). Urine dipstick shows mild hematuria. Renal ultrasound performed today confirms a 7 mm left lower pole nephrolithiasis with mild hydronephrosis. A percutaneous nephrostomy tube was successfully placed in the left collecting system; the patient reports significant pain relief and improved urine output since placement.

A: Primary diagnosis: obstructing left ureteral stone with mild hydronephrosis. Secondary considerations include obstructive pyelonephritis from the indwelling nephrostomy tube, hyperoxaluria (24‑hour urine oxalate 12 mg/kg), and potential complications of chronic steroid use. The patient’s ongoing anticoagulation (continuous LMWH) remains appropriate given his history of VTE. The stone is likely a calcium oxalate stone given his prior stone composition and elevated oxalate excretion.

P: 1. Continue percutaneous nephrostomy tube care; monitor output and catheter site for signs of infection.
2. Maintain current IV steroid regimen (methylprednisolone 125 mg q8h) and broad‑spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin 1.5 g q12h, cefepime 2 g q12h) until infectious workup stabilizes.
3. Continue continuous LMWH infusion; adjust dose per anti‑Xa levels.
4. Initiate aggressive IV hydration (150 mL/kg/24 h) and consider adding oral potassium citrate 30 mEq QID to reduce calcium oxalate supersaturation.
5. Obtain repeat serum creatinine, electrolytes, and CBC in 48 h; repeat renal ultrasound in 1 week or sooner if urine output falls.
6. Schedule definitive stone management (ureteroscopic lithotripsy with possible laser fragmentation) once infection and renal function stabilize.
7. Educate patient on signs of infection (fever, chills, worsening pain) and catheter complications; advise immediate return to ED if these occur.
8. Coordinate multidisciplinary care with GI (for ongoing steroid taper), hematology (for anticoagulation monitoring), and nephrology (for stone risk reduction).

Follow‑up: Return to urology clinic in 7 days for imaging review and counseling regarding definitive stone removal. Ongoing monitoring of renal function and anticoagulation therapy. Patient will be instructed to maintain high fluid intake and follow dietary recommendations for hyperoxaluria reduction.

Clinical Reasoning: The presence of a 7‑mm obstructing stone with mild hydronephrosis explains the acute flank pain. The placement of a percutaneous nephrostomy tube alleviated obstruction and improved pain; however, the tube introduces a potential nidus for infection, necessitating continued broad‑spectrum antibiotics. Persistent hyperoxaluria places the patient at high risk for recurrent stones, thus the addition of potassium citrate and aggressive hydration is warranted. Continued anticoagulation is essential given his recent VTE.

Disposition: Patient will remain on IV steroids, antibiotics, and LMWH while we observe for infection resolution and plan definitive stone treatment. The nephrostomy will remain indwelling until definitive stone removal is performed.
timeline.visit_scenario: Renal ultrasound confirms a 7 mm left lower pole stone causing mild hydronephrosis. A percutaneous nephrostomy tube is placed, relieving obstruction and pain. The patient reports significant pain relief.
test_results: US: 7 mm stone in left lower pole, hydronephrosis
timeline.patient_response: Pain reduced after nephrostomy; urine output improved.
timeline.disease_progression_notes: Obstructive stone resolved; potential infection risk mitigated.

timeline.note:
Chief Complaint: Suspected pulmonary embolism

History of Present Illness: A 35‑year‑old male with a complex medical history including ulcerative colitis, PBC, prior VTE, and a recent episode of severe colitis requiring ICU stay, presents with persistent cough, shortness of breath, and left lower lung opacity noted on chest imaging. He stopped rivaroxaban one week ago after a DVT one month prior and was on prophylactic LMWH. He reports progressive dyspnea and cough, but denies chest pain or hemoptysis.

Past Medical History: Ulcerative colitis, Iron deficiency anemia, Vitamin B12 deficiency, Primary sclerosing cholangitis, Venous thromboembolism history, Calcium oxalate kidney stones, Prior pulmonary embolism.

Current Medications: Oral mesalamine 4.8 g daily, Ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily, IV methylprednisolone 125 mg q8h, IV vancomycin 1.5 g q12h, IV cefepime 2 g q12h, LMWH continuous infusion, Monthly B12 injections, Acetaminophen 1 g q6h PRN, Oral potassium citrate 30 mEq QID.

Allergies: NKDA.

Physical Exam: BP 90/50, HR 130, RR 26, Temp 98.8°F, SpO2 88% on room air. Lungs: reduced breath sounds over left lower lobe, bibasilar crackles. Cardiac: tachycardic, no murmurs. Abdomen: soft, diffusely tender with guarding from recent colitis. Extremities: no peripheral edema.

Imaging: Chest X‑ray demonstrates a wedge‑shaped opacity in the left lower lobe. CT pulmonary angiogram confirms a large saddle pulmonary embolus.

Assessment: 1) Massive pulmonary embolism with hemodynamic instability (BP low, tachycardia, hypoxia). 2) Underlying hypercoagulable state related to inflammatory bowel disease and previous VTE. 3) Potential bleeding risk from recent high‑dose steroids, antibiotics, and anticoagulation.

Plan:
1. Initiate therapeutic unfractionated heparin drip, target aPTT 70‑90 sec. Continue LMWH infusion once hemodynamic status stabilizes and monitor anti‑Xa. 2. Continue broad‑spectrum IV antibiotics (vancomycin, cefepime) for ongoing colitis/endocarditis risk; adjust according to cultures and clinical course. 3. Continue IV methylprednisolone; consider taper once colitis is under control. 4. Monitor vitals every 30 min until stable, then hourly. Repeat CBC, BMP, coagulation profile and anti‑Xa levels q4h. 5. Supplemental O2 to maintain SpO2 >92%; consider proning if persistent hypoxia. 6. Consult Critical Care for anticoagulation management, hemodynamics, and potential need for thrombolysis if deterioration continues. 7. Repeat transthoracic echocardiography in 48 hrs to assess right heart strain. 8. Once stabilized, plan transition to therapeutic warfarin or DOAC (e.g., apixaban) after clearance of active infection and assessment of bleeding risk. 9. Continue current GI therapy; schedule repeat colonoscopy after infection control. 10. Counsel patient on VTE risk and adherence to anticoagulation.

Disposition: ICU admission continued; step‑down to intermediate care once BP >100/60 mmHg, HR <110 bpm, SpO2 >92 % on <4 L/min O2, and heparin aPTT goal achieved.

Follow‑up: Cardiology/Pulmonology consult within 24 hrs for anticoagulation strategy; GI follow‑up with colonoscopy in 4 weeks; rheumatology for hypercoagulability workup; regular CBC and platelet counts daily until discharge.
timeline.visit_scenario: Chest X‑ray shows a wedge‑shaped opacity. CT pulmonary angiogram confirms a large saddle PE. Anticoagulation therapy is escalated to a therapeutic heparin drip. Respiratory status improves with supportive care.
examination_findings: Reduced breath sounds left lower lung
test_results: Chest X‑ray: wedge‑shaped opacity left lower lobe
CT PE: large saddle pulmonary embolism
timeline.patient_response: Respiratory status improves after anticoagulation initiation.
timeline.disease_progression_notes: PE confirmed; anticoagulation transitioned to therapeutic; ongoing inflammatory crisis.

timeline.note:
Chief Complaint: Perforated ulcer and emergent colectomy.

History of Present Illness:
The patient is a 35‑year‑old male with a known history of ulcerative colitis (UC), primary sclerosing cholangitis, iron deficiency anemia, vitamin B12 deficiency, prior venous thromboembolism, and kidney stones. He presented to the ED with a rigid abdomen, spiking fever, and right lower quadrant pain. He was hypotensive, tachycardic, febrile, and hypoxic. Broad‑based sepsis was suspected, and a CT abdomen/pelvis was remarkable for portal venous gas and diffuse colonic wall thickening. The patient was taken to the operating room for emergent laparotomy. A 4‑cm perforated ulcer in the proximal ascending colon was identified; a Hartmann’s procedure was performed. The patient’s post‑operative course has been complicated by septic shock requiring ICU admission, mechanical ventilation, continuous heparin drip, and broad‑spectrum antibiotics.

Past Medical History: 
• Ulcerative colitis
• Primary sclerosing cholangitis
• Iron deficiency anemia
• Vitamin B12 deficiency
• Prior VTE
• Calcium oxalate kidney stones
• Known colonic perforation

Medications Prior to Admission:
• IV methylprednisolone 125 mg q8h
• Broad‑spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin, cefepime)
• LMWH continuous infusion
• IV heparin drip

Allergies: NKDA

Vital Signs (post‑op ICU, 08:30):
BP 80/45 mmHg, HR 140 bpm, Temp 103.7°F, RR 28/min, SpO2 86% on room air.

Physical Exam:
• Rigid abdomen with rebound tenderness.
• Purulent peritoneal fluid noted intra‑operatively.
• Current ICU status: intubated, on mechanical ventilation, on norepinephrine infusion.

Labs (post‑op CBC, CMP, CRP, lactate):
• WBC 30,000/µL
• CRP 70 mg/L
• Platelets 200,000/µL
• Lactate 4.5 mmol/L

Assessment:
1. Perforated ulcer of the proximal ascending colon with generalized peritonitis.
2. Septic shock secondary to intra‑abdominal infection.
3. Post‑operative complications: ongoing hemodynamic instability, ventilatory support, and concern for ongoing peritoneal contamination.
4. Known VTE; therapeutic anticoagulation continues.
5. Secondary inflammatory flare of UC; maintenance of steroids in ICU.

Plan:
• Continue broad‑spectrum IV antibiotics: vancomycin 1.5 g q12h, cefepime 2 g q12h, and metronidazole 500 mg q8h.
• Maintain therapeutic unfractionated heparin drip with aPTT monitoring; LMWH continuous infusion continued.
• Supportive care: vasopressor (norepinephrine) titration to MAP >65 mmHg, CRRT as needed.
• Daily CBC, CMP, lactate, and aPTT/anti‑Xa labs.
• Serial abdominal imaging (ultrasound/CT) to detect any leak or abscess.
• Consult infectious disease for antibiotic stewardship.
• Consult nutrition for enteral feeding planning; consider TPN if bowel rest required.
• Continue steroids (IV methylprednisolone 125 mg q8h) for underlying UC flare.
• ICU multidisciplinary rounds daily; plan for step‑down transfer when clinically stable, weaned off vasopressors, and weaned from ventilator.
• Review anticoagulation strategy with hematology when stable.
• Discuss goals of care with family; consider palliative options if ongoing organ failure.
• Schedule follow‑up colonoscopy once patient recovers from ICU stay and mucosal healing is adequate.

Disposition: ICU admission, mechanical ventilation, continuous heparin drip, and ongoing surgical and medical management.

Signature: Dr. [Name], General Surgery
timeline.visit_scenario: Emergency laparotomy reveals a 4 cm perforated ulcer in the proximal ascending colon. A Hartmann’s procedure is performed. Post‑op broad‑spectrum antibiotics are started, heparin drip continues, and the patient is transferred to ICU.
test_results: Surgical note: perforation in ascending colon
timeline.patient_response: Stabilized after surgery, but remains critically ill.
timeline.disease_progression_notes: Severe UC perforation → surgery, critical care; sepsis ongoing.

timeline.note:
Chief Complaint: Post‑operative ICU management

Subjective: 35‑year‑old male continues to be in ICU post‑laparotomy for perforated ulcer. He reports persistent fever, generalized pain, and decreased urine output. No new complaints since last visit.

Objective:
- Vitals: BP 88/52 mmHg; HR 122 bpm; Temp 101°F; RR 24/min; SpO2 92% on 0.3 L/min O2.
- Physical: Tachypnea, bilateral lung infiltrates, anasarca, mild pedal edema.
- Labs: Blood cultures positive for E.coli; serum creatinine 2.5 mg/dL; BUN 55 mg/dL; INR 1.6; fibrinogen 80 mg/dL; urine output 500 mL/24h.
- Imaging: Renal ultrasound shows no obstruction. Chest X‑ray confirms bilateral infiltrates.

Assessment:
1. Persistent septic shock secondary to E.coli bacteremia.
2. Acute kidney injury likely multifactorial – sepsis, nephrotoxic antimicrobial, hypotension.
3. Coagulopathy consistent with consumptive coagulopathy; fibrinogen low.
4. Post‑operative pulmonary complications – likely hospital‑acquired pneumonia.

Plan:
- Antimicrobials: Switch to piperacillin‑tazobactam 4.5g q6h (adjusted for CrCl); continue vancomycin with trough monitoring.
- Anticoagulation: Continue therapeutic unfractionated heparin drip; keep LMWH infusion; target aPTT 55‑70 sec.
- Renal support: Dialysis consult for CRRT; consider intermittent hemodialysis if urine output remains <400 mL/24h.
- Hemodynamic support: Continue vasopressor titration; aim MAP >65 mmHg.
- Fluid balance: Aggressive fluid resuscitation; daily weight and fluid balance charts.
- Ventilation: Gradual weaning trial; SBT daily.
- Monitoring: Labs q6h – CBC, CMP, coagulation, lactate. Monitor anti‑Xa q4h.
- Imaging: Repeat chest X‑ray every 48 hrs; renal US if obstruction suspected.
- Nutrition: Enteral nutrition via NG tube; consider parenteral if enteral insufficient.
- Follow‑up: ICU step‑down to high‑dependency unit when stable; repeat labs, cultures, and pulmonary status.

Disposition: Continue ICU care with above plan; reassess daily.
timeline.visit_scenario: Antibiotics are adjusted after blood cultures grow E. coli. Renal function is closely monitored; a nephrologist is involved. The patient remains on ventilatory support but is gradually improving.
timeline.patient_response: Temperature remains but gradually improves; kidney function slowly improves after dose adjustment.
timeline.disease_progression_notes: Post‑op septic shock with acute kidney injury; multi‑organ failure; requires multidisciplinary management.

timeline.note:
Visit #10 – 2023‑07‑17
Chief Complaint: Initiation of anti‑TNF therapy – first infliximab infusion

History of Present Illness:
35‑year‑old male with longstanding ulcerative colitis (UC) complicated by primary sclerosing cholangitis, iron deficiency anemia, vitamin B12 deficiency, recent VTE, recurrent calcium oxalate stones, and acute kidney injury. The patient reports ongoing diarrhea (10–12 loose stools daily), crampy abdominal pain, and low‑grade fever over the past 2 days. These symptoms persisted despite a tapering steroid regimen and broad‑spectrum antibiotics for recent septic episodes. Currently on LMWH for VTE prophylaxis and a heparin drip for transient severe PE.

Physical Examination:
Vital signs: BP 95/55 mmHg, HR 110/min, Temp 100.1°F, RR 20/min, SpO₂ 94% RA. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are unremarkable. Abdomen soft, mild diffuse tenderness without rebound. No hepatosplenomegaly. Skin shows no rashes. Extremities warm; peripheral pulses palpable.

Differential Diagnosis (post‑infusion):
1. Active UC flare necessitating biologic therapy.
2. Ongoing or superimposed infection (sepsis exacerbation or opportunistic infection).
3. Infliximab infusion reaction (clinical signs absent).
4. Acute kidney injury secondary to sepsis, renal ischemia, or drug effect.
5. Anticoagulation‑related complications (bleeding/hematologic).

Assessment:
- UC flare is the most probable cause of the symptoms, supported by the clinical course and lack of response to steroids.
- The patient’s low‑grade fever and tachycardia suggest a mild systemic inflammatory response, likely related to active colitis; however, infection cannot be ruled out.
- Infliximab trough level is 4.2 mg/L (below the therapeutic range of 5–7 mg/L for induction), indicating sub‑therapeutic exposure.
- Elevated creatinine (2.4 mg/dL) is concerning given recent septic episodes and nephrotoxic agents.

Plan:
1. Administer infliximab 5 mg/kg IV (first infusion) with diphenhydramine 50 mg IV premedication. Monitor vitals every 15 min for 4 h; watch for urticaria, hypotension, or bronchospasm.
2. Continue current steroid taper (methylprednisolone 125 mg q8h) until discharge.
3. Maintain antibiotic coverage with vancomycin 1.5 g q12h and cefepime 2 g q12h; obtain cultures repeatedly; consider de‑escalation once cultures return.
4. Continue LMWH at therapeutic dose; monitor anti‑Xa if needed.
5. Repeat CBC, CMP, CRP, and fecal calprotectin in 48 h.
6. Re‑check infliximab trough level 1 week post‑infusion to guide dose adjustment.
7. Renal function monitoring: hourly urine output, serum creatinine every 8 h; adjust nephrotoxic drugs accordingly.
8. Plan second infliximab infusion 2 weeks from today if clinical improvement; adjust dose to 10 mg/kg if trough remains sub‑therapeutic.
9. Schedule flexible sigmoidoscopy in 6 weeks once the patient is clinically stable to assess mucosal healing.
10. Continue VTE prophylaxis per current protocol; repeat DVT/PE imaging if clinical concern arises.

Disposition: Admit to GI service for close monitoring of response to infliximab and to manage potential adverse events.

Follow‑up recommendations: Will be summarized in separate section below.
timeline.visit_scenario: The first infusion of infliximab is administered with premedication. Patient remains hemodynamically stable with no infusion reaction.
timeline.disease_progression_notes: Biologic therapy initiated; monitor for infections and drug reactions.

timeline.note:
**Chief Complaint:** Purpuric rash and itchiness on lower limbs.

**History of Present Illness:**
35‑year‑old male with a complex medical history including inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), chronic anticoagulation, and recent septic shock, presents with a 2‑day history of progressively worsening itchy, palpable purpura on the shins. The rash began 5 days ago after the recent first infusion of infliximab and is now involving both lower legs. The patient reports discomfort but denies fever, arthralgia, abdominal pain, or urinary symptoms.

**Past Medical History:** IBD-associated anemia, iron deficiency anemia, B12 deficiency, colorectal cancer risk, VTE, primary sclerosing cholangitis, calcium oxalate kidney stones, acute severe ulcerative colitis flare, acute kidney injury, septic shock, hospital‑acquired pneumonia, perforated ulcer, previous pulmonary embolus, and multiple recent surgeries.

**Medications:**
- Oral mesalamine 4.8 g daily
- Ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily
- IV methylprednisolone 125 mg q8h (now transitioned to oral prednisone 40 mg daily)
- IV vancomycin 1.5 g q12h
- IV piperacillin‑tazobactam 4.5 g q6h
- IV cefepime 2 g q12h
- LMWH continuous infusion
- Monthly B12 injections
- Acetaminophen 1 g q6h PRN
- Oral potassium citrate 30 mEq QID
- Unfractionated heparin drip
- IV metronidazole 500 mg q8h
- Diphenhydramine 50 mg IV
- Infliximab 5 mg/kg IV (held pending rash resolution)
- IV fluids, CRRT, analgesia as needed

**Allergies:** NKDA.

**Social History:** Non‑smoker. No alcohol or illicit drug use.

**Review of Systems:** Negative for fever, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, new-onset abdominal pain, urinary symptoms, or systemic rash elsewhere.

**Vitals at Encounter:**
- BP 92/54 mmHg
- HR 110 bpm
- Temp 99 °F
- RR 18/min
- O2 Sat 96% RA

**Physical Examination:**
- Skin: Multiple palpable purpuric papules and plaques on both shins, tender to palpation. No ulcerations or necrosis.
- Other systems: Unremarkable.

**Diagnostics:**
- Skin biopsy: Inflammatory changes consistent with leukocytoclastic vasculitis.
- Complement panel: Low C4; C3 normal (not provided).
- Serology: ANCA negative.

**Assessment:**
The presentation is most consistent with small‑vessel leukocytoclastic vasculitis, likely drug‑induced (post‑infliximab) or IBD‑associated extra‑intestinal manifestation. Low C4 raises the possibility of hypocomplementemic urticarial vasculitis syndrome (HUVS), though ANA and cryoglobulin were not yet obtained. ANCA‑negative rules out ANCA‑associated vasculitis. The patient’s severe medical comorbidities and active anticoagulation require careful monitoring for bleeding.

**Plan:**
1. Continue oral prednisone 40 mg daily, with a taper over 6–8 weeks as rash resolves.
2. Hold biologic therapy (infliximab) until complete resolution of cutaneous lesions.
3. Continue anticoagulation (LMWH continuous infusion + unfractionated heparin drip) with close monitoring for bleeding complications.
4. Order CBC with platelet count, CMP, CRP, ESR next visit to assess systemic inflammation.
5. Repeat complement (C3/C4) and consider ANA, anti‑dsDNA, cryoglobulin if rash persists beyond 2 weeks.
6. Counsel on signs of bleeding, wound care, and when to seek emergent care.
7. Initiate antihistamine (cetirizine 10 mg PO daily) for residual pruritus.
8. Schedule follow‑up in 7 days; earlier if worsening.
9. Coordinate with rheumatology if rash is refractory or systemic features develop.
10. Document skin changes and photograph if lesion progression for future comparison.

**Disposition:** Patient is stable for discharge with instructions to monitor for bleeding and to return immediately if lesions worsen or new systemic symptoms appear.
timeline.visit_scenario: Skin biopsy confirms leukocytoclastic vasculitis. Treatment switches to oral prednisone and temporary hold of infliximab.
examination_findings: Purpuric papules on shins, palpable purpura
timeline.patient_response: Rash improved over 5 days; patient reports reduced itchiness.
timeline.disease_progression_notes: Autoimmune vasculitis likely drug‑induced; managed with steroids while monitoring for infection.

timeline.reason_for_visit: Colorectal cancer surveillance and surgical management
timeline.note:
Chief Complaint: Surveillance for colorectal cancer and surgical management. 35‑year‑old male with ulcerative colitis, PSC, high‑grade dysplasia, prior surgeries, and current anticoagulation for VTE.  He reports mild abdominal discomfort with no recent change in stool frequency or character.

History of Present Illness: Colonoscopy (07/27) identified a 15‑mm polyp at the ileocolic anastomosis. Biopsy was suspicious for malignant neoplasm. CT abdomen/pelvis (07/29) demonstrated no metastatic disease. The patient is anxious but has consented to subtotal colectomy with loop ileostomy. He is on infliximab infusion, LMWH (40 mg SC q12h), IV steroids taper, oral prednisone, and a course of antibiotics.

Vital Signs: BP 90/50, HR 112, Temp 98.7 °F, RR 20, SpO₂ 96% on room air.

Physical Examination: Afebrile. Mild left lower quadrant tenderness on palpation. No ascites or peritoneal signs.

Labs: CBC shows Hb 8.5 g/dL. CMP reveals Cr 2.3 mg/dL indicating AKI. Full metabolic panel otherwise stable.

Assessment: Suspicious colorectal malignancy at the ileocolic anastomosis. No metastatic disease currently. Likely high‑grade dysplasia progressing to adenocarcinoma in a patient with ulcerative colitis and PSC. The patient is at increased risk for operative complications and post‑operative renal dysfunction.

Plan:
1. **Surgical** – Proceed with subtotal colectomy and loop ileostomy. Intra‑operative antibiotics with cefazolin 2 g IV (add vancomycin if wound contamination suspected). LMWH continued peri‑op with bridging to therapeutic dosing.
2. **Medications** – Continue oral prednisone 10 mg daily (taper post‑op). Hold infliximab until recovery. Continue antibiotics as ordered.
3. **Renal** – Optimize hydration, hold nephrotoxic agents. Repeat CMP and eGFR daily.
4. **Post‑op Care** – ICU admission post‑op for close monitoring of vitals, renal function, and early detection of ileus or bleeding. Start ondansetron 4 mg IV q8h for nausea.
5. **Cancer Staging** – Pathology from resection will guide staging; arrange pre‑op CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (already negative) and consider CEA if available.
6. **Stoma Care** – Educate patient on ostomy appliance and self‑care; arrange stoma nurse consultation.
7. **Oncology** – Order pre‑op oncology consult for staging and discussion of adjuvant therapy if indicated.

Follow‑up: Post‑op day 1 surgical ICU; postoperative day 7 surgical clinic; oncology outpatient visit 2 weeks after surgery; stoma reversal evaluation 4–6 weeks post‑op when wound healing is adequate.

Patient was counseled regarding operative risks, benefits, postoperative course, and long‑term surveillance. He verbalized understanding and consented to the plan.
timeline.visit_scenario: Colonoscopy reveals 15 mm polyp at ileocolic anastomosis; pathology confirms adenocarcinoma pT3N1. A decision is made for subtotal colectomy with loop ileostomy. Pre‑op antibiotics and anticoagulation plan are finalized.

test_results: Colonoscopy: 15 mm polyp at ileocolic anastomosis
treatments_administered: Plan subtotal colectomy with loop ileostomy
timeline.patient_response: Patient anxious but agrees to surgery; understands risks.
timeline.disease_progression_notes: Colonic carcinoma confirmed; urgent colectomy indicated; continue aggressive medical therapy; consider adjuvant chemo later.

Chief Complaint: Vague lower abdominal cramp.
History of Present Illness: 35‑year‑old male presents with intermittent lower abdominal cramping, frequent loose stools, and greenish mucus discharge for the past few days. No fever, vomiting, or weight loss reported. He denies recent travel or dietary changes.
Past Medical History: Inflammatory Bowel Disease–associated anemia, iron deficiency anemia, vitamin B12 deficiency, primary sclerosing cholangitis (mild elevation), colorectal cancer risk, venous thromboembolism, calcium oxalate kidney stones.
Medications: None. 
Allergies: NKDA.
Physical Examination: Vitals stable – BP 110/70, HR 82, Temp 98.4°F, RR 16, O2 98%. Abdomen mild tenderness in the right lower quadrant without rebound or guarding. Bowel sounds normal. Cardiovascular, respiratory, and extremity exams unremarkable.
Labs: CBC shows Hb 10.9 g/dL, WBC 6.8k/µL. CRP 5 mg/L. CMP within normal limits.
Assessment: Likely flare of underlying inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn’s disease or ulcerative colitis) presenting with abdominal cramp, mucus in stool, and mild inflammation. Differential includes infectious colitis, diverticulitis, appendicitis, functional abdominal pain, and colonic polyps.
Plan:
1. Initiate extended‑release mesalamine 2.4 g PO daily (symptomatic therapy for IBD flare). Counsel patient on importance of adherence; discuss potential alternatives (e.g., sulfasalazine) if cost or compliance is an issue.
2. Symptom management: Stay hydrated, avoid potential gut irritants (spicy, fried foods), consider low‑residue diet if stool frequency is high.
3. Address anemia: start oral iron supplement ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily; advise monitoring for GI side effects.
4. Monitor disease activity: CBC and CRP repeated in 4 weeks. Consider fecal calprotectin if symptoms persist after 2–4 weeks.
5. Follow‑up: Return to clinic in 4 weeks for reassessment; if symptoms worsen or show signs of peritonitis, call office or go to ER promptly.
6. Referral: Gastroenterology consultation if inadequate response to mesalamine or if new GI symptoms develop.
7. Colon cancer surveillance: Schedule colonoscopy in 1 year per colorectal cancer risk protocol.
8. Educate patient on recognizing red‑flag symptoms (fever, worsening pain, blood per rectum, weight loss).
diagnoses_considered: Inflammatory bowel disease flare (Crohn’s disease/ulcerative colitis)
follow_up_recommendations: Return in 4 weeks for CBC and CRP repeat; reassess abdominal symptoms.
If symptoms worsen or new red‑flag signs appear, seek urgent care or ER.
Refer to gastroenterology if inadequate response to mesalamine.
Schedule colonoscopy in 1 year due to colorectal cancer risk.

Chief Complaint: Severe bloody diarrhea and dehydration.

History of Present Illness: 35‑year‑old male presents to the ED with 12 episodes of bright red blood per day, low‑grade fever (99.9 °F), abdominal pain, and signs of dehydration. Patient reports intermittent lower abdominal cramping the preceding day. He admits to non‑adherence with extended‑release mesalamine 2.4 g PO daily. Vitals on arrival: BP 95/57 mmHg, HR 110 bpm, RR 22 /min, O₂ sat 96 % RA, temperature 99.9 °F. Physical exam reveals thready pulses, pale conjunctiva, abdominal tenderness without guarding, and digital rectal exam shows fresh blood.

Labs: hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, WBC 18.6 k/µL, CRP 38 mg/L, ESR 94 mm/hr, PT/INR 1.2. Stool C. diff toxin and stool cultures are pending. CT abdomen/pelvis (non‑contrast) shows diffuse colonic wall thickening 10‑12 mm without perforation and a 7 mm proximal ureteric stone.

Assessment: 1. Severe flare of inflammatory bowel disease (likely ulcerative colitis) presenting with massive bloody diarrhea, anemia, leukocytosis, and elevated inflammatory markers, precipitated by medication non‑adherence. 2. Dehydration and mild sepsis. 3. Incidental 7 mm proximal ureteric stone; likely asymptomatic but warrants monitoring.

Plan:
1. Admit to gastroenterology service.
2. Initiate IV methylprednisolone 125 mg q8h for 3 days, then taper based on clinical response.
3. Continue high‑dose oral mesalamine 4.8 g daily until discharge.
4. Begin IV fluid resuscitation: 1.5 L bolus, maintenance 125 mL/hr, with urine output monitoring >1 mL/kg/hr.
5. Prophylactic LMWH 40 mg SC daily for VTE prevention.
6. Repeat CBC, CMP, CRP, ESR every 48 h.
7. Await stool culture results; consider empiric broad‑spectrum antibiotics if gram‑negative/anaerobic organisms are identified.
8. Urology consult for 7 mm proximal ureteral stone; anticipate spontaneous passage but monitor pain and renal function.
9. Discuss dietary modifications (low‑residue, adequate fluid) and strict adherence to maintenance mesalamine. 10. Counsel on signs of worsening infection or DVT.

Disposition: Observation in inpatient setting until clinical status stabilizes; prepare for possible escalation (e.g., biologic therapy) if refractory.

Follow‑up: Outpatient GI clinic in 1 week or sooner if symptoms worsen. Labs: CBC, CMP, CRP, ESR, fecal calprotectin. Continue maintenance mesalamine 2.4 g daily and ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily. Reinforce medication adherence and review dietitian and behavioral support options.

Chief Complaint: Severe bloody diarrhea and dehydration, ICU admission required.

History of Present Illness: 35‑y/o male with known inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) presents to the GI floor with persistent bloody diarrhea, fever (100.2 °F), abdominal pain and signs of dehydration. The patient has been on chronic high‑dose mesalamine, oral iron, monthly B12 injections, and IV methylprednisolone 125 mg q8h for acute flare. The current admission began after a severe diarrheal episode in the ED on 07/02, during which the patient received IV steroids, fluids, high‑dose mesalamine, and LMWH. On day 1 of GI floor admission, fluid resuscitation and IV iron infusion 200 mg were administered. No complications were noted.

Review of Systems: Positive for persistent bloody diarrhea, fever, abdominal pain, and signs of dehydration. No urinary symptoms, chest pain or shortness of breath.

Physical Examination: Vitals: BP 100/58, HR 104, Temp 100.2 °F, RR 20, O₂ 97% RA. General: appears ill, pale mucous membranes. Abdomen: diffuse colonic tenderness, no rebound or guarding. Digital rectal exam: bright red blood.

Labs: High inflammatory markers with CRP 35 mg/L, WBC 18.7 k/µL. Hemoglobin 9.5 g/dL, platelets 210 k/µL. Coagulation: PT/INR 1.3, INR 1.4, fibrinogen 80 mg/dL. Stool cultures bland; C. diff toxin negative.

Assessment: Exacerbation of IBD (likely ulcerative colitis flare) causing acute severe colitis with hypovolemia. Dehydration corrected with IV fluids. Steroid therapy is ongoing; IV methylpred 125 mg q8h. Mesalamine high‑dose 4.8 g PO continues. Patient’s VTE history and current steroid therapy justify LMWH prophylaxis.

Differential:
1. Severe acute ulcerative colitis flare
2. IBD flare with severe colitis despite steroids (possible steroid‑refractory)
3. Infectious colitis (C. diff ruled out, cultures bland)
4. Ischemic colitis secondary to high blood loss/dehydration (unlikely but considered)
5. PSC‑associated colitis flare
6. Medication‑induced colitis secondary to mesalamine (rare)

Plan:
1. Continue IV methylpred 125 mg q8h and taper per inpatient protocol.
2. Continue high‑dose oral mesalamine 4.8 g daily.
3. Continue LMWH 40 mg SC daily.
4. Continue IV iron 200 mg and consider repeat infusion or transition to oral iron contingent on tolerance.
5. Monitor urine output, vitals, stool frequency, and abdominal pain hourly.
6. Repeat CBC, CMP, CRP, ESR, and coagulation profile in 48 h.
7. If inadequate response after 48‑72 h of steroids, consider escalation to biologic therapy (infliximab or adalimumab) and/or tocilizumab if severe.
8. Review abdominal imaging if clinical deterioration; consider CT enterography to rule out ischemia.
9. Post‑discharge: arrange endoscopy for evaluation of mucosal healing and colorectal cancer screening per risk protocol.
10. Provide dietary counseling: high‑potassium diet, liquid meals until stool frequency improves.

Disposition: Planned for discharge after 3‑day inpatient stay pending stabilization; patient will receive outpatient follow‑up in GI clinic within 2 weeks.

Patient education: Discussed importance of adherence to steroids and mesalamine, monitoring for signs of infection or steroid adverse effects, and importance of LMWH despite discontinuation of therapy post‑discharge. Advised to seek immediate care if fever >101 °F, abdominal pain worsens, or stool >10 bloody."

notes.content:
Chief Complaint: Acute febrile illness with peritoneal signs; concern for bowel perforation/necrotizing colitis.

History of Present Illness: The patient is a 35‑year‑old male with known ulcerative colitis (mild‑moderate) complicated by PSC and increased risk for colorectal cancer. He has been on mesalamine 2.4 g PO daily and methylprednisolone 125 mg IV q8h for the past 48 h. Today, at 22:00, he developed a sudden spike in temperature to 103.2 °F with rigid abdomen, rebound tenderness, nausea and hypotension. The patient reports 5–7 episodes of bright red stools since admission. He denies chest pain or dyspnea. No new medications or foods.

Past Medical History: IBD, PSC, VTE, iron deficiency anemia, vitamin B12 deficiency, calcium oxalate stones, IBD‑associated anemia, colorectal cancer surveillance.

Review of Systems: Positive for fever, chills, abdominal pain, nausea, decreased urinary output. Negative for chest pain, shortness of breath, orthopnea, headache.

Physical Examination: General: Pale, diaphoretic, tachypneic. Vital signs: BP 88/52 mmHg, HR 120 bpm, RR 24 /min, Temp 103.2 °F, SpO₂ 94 % RA. Abdomen: distended, rigid, with marked guarding and rebound tenderness throughout. Bowel sounds decreased. Skin warm. Cardiovascular: tachycardia, faint radial pulses. No JVD or peripheral edema. Neurologic: alert, oriented.

Labs: WBC 25 k/µL with neutrophilia, CRP 60 mg/L, INR 1.4, fibrinogen 70 mg/dL, lactate 3.2 mmol/L (pending). Blood cultures negative.

Radiology: CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast shows portal venous gas, moderate perihepatic free fluid (approx. 2 cm), and diffuse colonic wall thickening. No obvious perforation site identified.

Assessment: The presentation most consistent with a perforated viscus or necrotizing colitis secondary to severe ulcerative colitis flare, or possibly mesenteric ischemia secondary to the patient’s VTE history. Portal venous gas and free fluid raise high suspicion for bowel perforation. The patient’s hemodynamic instability mandates ICU management.

Differential Diagnosis:
1. Perforated ulcerative colitis (ischemic/necrotizing).
2. Mesenteric ischemia (arterial or venous).
3. Infectious colitis (C. difficile, bacterial).
4. Abdominal sepsis secondary to portal venous gas.
5. Transient mesenteric venous thrombosis.
6. Delayed presentation of diverticulitis.

Plan:
- Continue current broad-spectrum antibiotics (IV vancomycin 1.5 g q12h, cefepime 2 g q12h). Consider adding metronidazole 500 mg q8h pending cultures if anaerobic coverage needed.
- Steroids: Continue methylprednisolone 125 mg q8h for IBD flare; hold only if surgical definitive intervention is required.
- Fluid resuscitation: Continue 2 L NS, adjust to urine output > 0.5 mL/kg/h; consider vasopressor support if hypotension persists (norepinephrine).
- Continue LMWH continuous infusion pending repeat coagulation profile.
- Surgical consult: Immediate evaluation by general surgery for possible laparotomy and source control. Notify anesthesia for emergent operation criteria.
- Gastroenterology: Continue mesalamine 2.4 g PO daily; consider escalation if flare persists.
- ICU monitoring: Hourly vitals, urine output, continuous cardiac monitoring.
- Repeat labs: CBC, CMP, lactate, coagulation panel q6h; blood cultures repeat if clinical condition worsens.
- Imaging: Repeat CT abdomen/pelvis in 24 h or sooner if clinical deterioration.
- Nutrition: NPO, early enteral nutrition if stable post-operatively.
- VTE prophylaxis: Continue LMWH; switch to therapeutic dose if thrombus confirmed.
- Family education: Discuss potential surgical intervention, risks of bowel resection, and need for post‑operative monitoring.

Disposition: ICU admission pending surgical consult. Pending operative management.

Follow‑up: Post‑operative ICU course, step‑down to medicine floor when stable, colonoscopy per surveillance protocol within 3‑6 months. Close monitoring of CBC, iron status, and vitamin B12 levels.

notes.content:
**Chief Complaint:** Endoscopic evaluation of colitis.

**History of Present Illness:**
The patient is a 35‑year‑old male with a history of ulcerative colitis (UC), primary sclerosing cholangitis, iron deficiency anemia, B12 deficiency, and prior venous thromboembolism. He presented with persistent bloody stools, abdominal pain, and fever and has been hospitalized for a severe UC flare. On 2023‑07‑04 he was in the ICU for septic shock and portal venous gas suspicious for a perforation. The current day we performed a flexible sigmoidoscopy to assess mucosal severity.

**Physical Examination:**
- BP 94/55 mmHg, HR 118/min, Temp 102.5 °F, RR 22/min, SpO₂ 95% on room air.
- Abdomen diffuse tenderness with rebound; no obvious guarding on exam.
- Stool: 6 bright red/blood‑stained stools/day.

**Procedure Summary:**
Flexible sigmoidoscopy revealed markedly ulcerated, friable mucosa with multiple pseudomembranes. Biopsies were obtained.

**Pathology:**
- Inflammation with active colitis; no crypt architectural distortion or dysplasia.

**Laboratory Results:**
- CRP 55 mg/L (↑), WBC 22 000/µL (↑), hemoglobin 9.5 g/dL, platelets 420 000/µL, INR 1.4.
- Stool culture pending; C. difficile PCR sent.

**Assessment:**
The patient remains in a severe UC flare complicated by possible superimposed pseudomembranous colitis. The portal venous gas and free fluid raise concern for ischemia or perforation, though the bowel is not yet perforated per imaging. The patient remains hemodynamically unstable but responsive to fluid resuscitation.

**Differential Diagnosis:**
1. Severe active ulcerative colitis flare with pseudomembranous colitis.
2. Clostridioides difficile infection.
3. Ischemic colitis / early perforation indicated by portal venous gas.
4. Septic shock secondary to colitis.
5. Medication‑induced mucosal injury (high‑dose steroids/mesalamine).

**Plan:**
- **IV Methylprednisolone 125 mg q8h** – continue until clinical improvement.
- **IV vancomycin 1.5 g q12h** – continue; consider adding oral vancomycin PO for C. difficile pending PCR.
- **IV cefepime 2 g q12h** – continue for broad coverage.
- **LMWH continuous infusion** – continue for VTE prophylaxis.
- **High‑dose oral mesalamine 4.8 g daily** – continue.
- **Oral iron and monthly B12 injections** – continue.
- Monitor vitals q4h, abdominal exam q6h.
- Repeat CBC, CRP, and basic metabolic panel daily.
- Obtain abdominal CT with contrast next morning to evaluate for perforation or ischemia.
- Consult general surgery immediately; consider laparotomy if perforation confirmed.
- Consider initiating anti‑TNF therapy (infliximab) if no improvement after 48‑72 h and imaging is reassuring.

**Disposition:**
Patient remains in ICU for close monitoring. If vitals stabilize, plan a step‑down to a regular ward with continued IV steroids and antibiotics. Discharge planning contingent on resolution of systemic symptoms and mucosal healing. The patient will be followed by gastroenterology in 3‑4 days or sooner if condition changes.

**Patient Education:**
Advised about the risks of severe colitis, importance of medication adherence, signs of perforation (severe sudden pain, worsening vitals), and when to seek immediate care.

Chief Complaint: Right flank pain, nausea, abnormal urination.

S: Patient is a 35‑year‑old male with a history of ulcerative colitis, primary sclerosing cholangitis, venous thromboembolism, and recurrent calcium oxalate kidney stones. He reports right flank pain that has been present for the past 24 hours, associated with nausea and mild urinary frequency. He denies fever, chills, gross hematuria, or recent trauma.

O: Vitals are stable with a blood pressure of 102/58 mmHg, heart rate 110/min, temperature 98.6°F, respiratory rate 18/min, and SpO2 98% on room air. Examination reveals right flank tenderness to palpation; left flank and abdomen are otherwise benign. Peripheral pulses are intact. No abdominal guarding or rebound. Mild leukocytosis noted on CBC (WBC 9.5 ×10^9/L). Urine dipstick shows mild hematuria. Renal ultrasound performed today confirms a 7 mm left lower pole nephrolithiasis with mild hydronephrosis. A percutaneous nephrostomy tube was successfully placed in the left collecting system; the patient reports significant pain relief and improved urine output since placement.

A: Primary diagnosis: obstructing left ureteral stone with mild hydronephrosis. Secondary considerations include obstructive pyelonephritis from the indwelling nephrostomy tube, hyperoxaluria (24‑hour urine oxalate 12 mg/kg), and potential complications of chronic steroid use. The patient’s ongoing anticoagulation (continuous LMWH) remains appropriate given his history of VTE. The stone is likely a calcium oxalate stone given his prior stone composition and elevated oxalate excretion.

P: 1. Continue percutaneous nephrostomy tube care; monitor output and catheter site for signs of infection.
2. Maintain current IV steroid regimen (methylprednisolone 125 mg q8h) and broad‑spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin 1.5 g q12h, cefepime 2 g q12h) until infectious workup stabilizes.
3. Continue continuous LMWH infusion; adjust dose per anti‑Xa levels.
4. Initiate aggressive IV hydration (150 mL/kg/24 h) and consider adding oral potassium citrate 30 mEq QID to reduce calcium oxalate supersaturation.
5. Obtain repeat serum creatinine, electrolytes, and CBC in 48 h; repeat renal ultrasound in 1 week or sooner if urine output falls.
6. Schedule definitive stone management (ureteroscopic lithotripsy with possible laser fragmentation) once infection and renal function stabilize.
7. Educate patient on signs of infection (fever, chills, worsening pain) and catheter complications; advise immediate return to ED if these occur.
8. Coordinate multidisciplinary care with GI (for ongoing steroid taper), hematology (for anticoagulation monitoring), and nephrology (for stone risk reduction).

Follow‑up: Return to urology clinic in 7 days for imaging review and counseling regarding definitive stone removal. Ongoing monitoring of renal function and anticoagulation therapy. Patient will be instructed to maintain high fluid intake and follow dietary recommendations for hyperoxaluria reduction.

Clinical Reasoning: The presence of a 7‑mm obstructing stone with mild hydronephrosis explains the acute flank pain. The placement of a percutaneous nephrostomy tube alleviated obstruction and improved pain; however, the tube introduces a potential nidus for infection, necessitating continued broad‑spectrum antibiotics. Persistent hyperoxaluria places the patient at high risk for recurrent stones, thus the addition of potassium citrate and aggressive hydration is warranted. Continued anticoagulation is essential given his recent VTE.

Disposition: Patient will remain on IV steroids, antibiotics, and LMWH while we observe for infection resolution and plan definitive stone treatment. The nephrostomy will remain indwelling until definitive stone removal is performed.

Obstructive pyelonephritis secondary to nephrostomy tube
